what_it_does: A design-capture and unification document for a proposed composable concurrency surface in the Company system. It records an expanded vision of how concurrency is exposed, configured, viewed, and leveraged; distinguishes already-built substrate from net-new capabilities; and folds in same-day research findings on RHM integration, canvas grouping, and broader system seams.
observations: Uses epistemic tags Built / Net-new / Open and a run:// addressing scheme throughout.
Proposes an input-driven allocator registry with strategies such as by-size, by-file, by-tag, by-tag+size, by-cluster, by-list, and by-count.
Models a concurrent step as a vertical segmented bar and a multi-step process as a left-to-right timeline of columns with inputs/outputs in the gaps.
Defines three selection altitudes: column (step), segment (role-type), and run (individual run).
Claims the Pulse->River->Nodes cognition view and the Timeline->Column->Segment->Run sequencer are two render-modes of the same run:// substrate.
Folds three research threads with verdicts: RHM integration is reuse, canvas timeline is a substrate fork needing a Tim decision, broader seams are mostly connected.
Lists an explicit integration order from landing the branch through allocator, backend expose, FE render-modes, and GPU actuators.
Honest about gaps: tools in role authoring, allocator priority, step vs turn, queue semantics, cross-step I/O, mode->loadout actuator, and always-on drivers.
standouts: The input-driven allocator generalizes existing fanout patterns (cast and draws) by computing N runs from resolved input rather than using a fixed count.
The timeline/sequencer is framed as a render-mode of the existing run://-addressed substrate, not a separate concurrency system.
Research verdict that integration is mostly reuse on a shared substrate, with the key net-new unlock being a swarm-step node-type that turns pipelines into graphs.
Strong epistemic honesty: every claim is tagged Built, Net-new, or Open, and GPU/lifecycle decisions are explicitly gated as needs-tim.
conventions: Epistemic tagging of claims as Built, Net-new, or Open.
run:// universal addressing for turns, roles, and outputs.
Reflects-never-owns principle for rendering live data.
File-local, neutral tone with no ranking or winner language.
Inline code references using file:line citations.
Status markers, timestamps, and an explicit integration-order dependency chain.
concerns: The document is dense, mixing vision, allocator design, visual model, research findings, and integration planning in one file.
Several needs-tim decisions are deferred while the doc already asserts integration order and verdicts, which may prejudge Tim's steer.
Jargon such as L1/no-text-wall law, claude-p floor, and RHM_VERB_SPECS is used without in-file definition.
The symbols list is empty despite many code references, making this file hard to link automatically.
It claims integration is overwhelmingly reuse while still listing substantial net-new engine, backend, and FE work.
notes: A design-capture doc that maps a proposed input-partitioned concurrency allocator and timeline visualization onto the existing run:// substrate, with honest built/net-new labels and a researched integration plan.
questions: What is the exact data-model distinction between a step and a turn?
How does the allocator attach to the existing role schema and propose_role API?
What are the precise semantics of cross-step I/O gaps: join, reduce, filter, or pass-through?
Which substrate will the timeline use: the tldraw board or the SVG River, and what are the trade-offs for multi-select?
How is the claude-p floor and RHM_VERB_SPECS whitelist defined and enforced outside this file?
What queue policy applies when wanted runs exceed the slot knee: FIFO, priority, or per-role reservation?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches — the stated purpose is to capture and unify Tim's vision, expand it, state built vs net-new, and frame adjacent-system research. The file does all of that and additionally lands research findings and proposes a concrete integration order.
apparent_themes: Substrate unification through a single run://-addressed event log.
Epistemic honesty in labeling built, net-new, and open items.
Resource-aware concurrency using slot budgets and wanted/running/queued counts.
Generalization over specialization: one allocator subsumes cast and draws.
Render-mode equivalence between spatial graph and temporal sequencer.
intention_signals: To consolidate a vision for exposing, configuring, viewing, and leveraging concurrency into one composable surface that reuses the existing run:// substrate while honestly scoping the net-new engine, visualization, and integration work.
novelty: Distinct in treating a concurrent step as N runs computed by an input-partitioning allocator and visualized as a segmented bar/timeline, explicitly framed as a generalization of existing fanout and a render-mode of the same cognition substrate.
contribution: The allocator strategy registry concept, the three-altitude visual model, the honest Built/Net-new/Open taxonomy, the research-thread folding format, the integration-order dependency chain, and the unification insight that concurrency pipelines are run://-addressed graphs.
summary_for_embedding: Design document for a composable concurrency surface proposing an input-driven allocator registry, a segmented-bar timeline visualization, and integration with the RHM and node graph, distinguishing built substrate from net-new engine and FE work.
intention_for_embedding: To capture and unify Tim's vision of how concurrency is exposed and leveraged across the Company system while honestly scoping what exists, what must be built, and what research is needed.
